title: The Kitchen
text: She kept the radio on while she cooked, not for the music but for the company of voices. The onions went translucent in the pan. Outside the window the yard was getting dark and the motion light kept triggering for the cat. She added garlic, then tomatoes from a can, and the kitchen filled with a smell that made the house feel smaller and warmer. Her daughter was upstairs doing homework or pretending to. Her husband was not coming home again. She stirred the sauce and tasted it from the wooden spoon and added salt. When the pasta was done she called upstairs twice and then a third time. They ate together at the table without talking, which was fine. The food was good. That was enough for a Wednesday.

title: Sacramento
text: I drove through Sacramento once in August and the heat was a physical thing, a weight on the car. The air conditioning had stopped working outside Stockton and I had all the windows down and my shirt was soaked through. I pulled off the interstate for gas and stood in the parking lot of a station that sold live bait and lottery tickets. A woman inside was arguing with someone on the phone in a language I did not recognize. I bought a bottle of water and a bag of ice and sat in the car with the ice on my lap, melting through my jeans. The highway went on after that for a long time. I do not remember where I was going. I remember the ice.

title: The Dog
text: The dog had three legs and no interest in sympathy. She ran faster than most four-legged dogs and stole food from the counter with a precision that suggested planning. I found her under a truck in a casino parking lot in Reno, covered in motor oil and missing most of her left ear. The vet said she was about two. She lived to be fifteen. In her last year she slept on a orthopedic bed that cost more than my first car and I carried her outside three times a day to stand in the grass. She would lift her face to the wind and close her eyes and I would stand there holding her and think about nothing at all. The morning she died I sat on the kitchen floor for an hour. Then I washed her bowl and put it in the cabinet and closed the door.

title: The Exam
text: You fail the exam twice. The third time you arrive early and sit in the parking garage with the manual open on the steering wheel, reading the same paragraph about commercial zoning requirements over and over. The testing room is cold and smells like carpet cleaner. The woman next to you is chewing gum so loudly you can hear it through the silence. You finish with eleven minutes left and check your answers and change two of them, which is a mistake. You pass by a single point. Your mother calls that evening and you tell her and she says she always knew you would. You hang up the phone and sit on the edge of the bed and look at your hands. You have been so afraid and now it is over and you feel nothing.

title: Snow
text: The first time he sees snow he is twenty-six years old. He has come from a place where the coldest day is still warm enough to sleep outside. He stands in the parking lot of a grocery store in Michigan and watches it fall and cannot move. A man walks past and says something about the weather and he cannot answer. The flakes are enormous, the size of coins, and they land on his jacket and he can see the structure of each one before it melts. He understands then what people mean when they say the world can be transformed overnight. The next morning the cars are buried and the trees look like they have been dipped in white paint. He walks to work because the buses are not running. It takes an hour. His shoes are ruined. He is so happy he is shaking.

title: The Fight
text: We fought about the dishes, which was not about the dishes. It was about her mother or it was about money or it was about the fact that I had said something at dinner three weeks ago that I could not remember but she could quote exactly. She had a talent for remembering the worst thing you ever said and holding it at the ready like a knife. I had a talent for going quiet, which was worse. We stood in the kitchen and she talked and I said nothing and after a while she threw a glass against the wall. It did not shatter dramatically. It bounced off the drywall and rolled under the table. We both looked at it. She started laughing first. I did too. We cleaned it up together and went to bed and the next morning the argument was still there, waiting, like a patient animal.

title: The Lake
text: My grandfather took me fishing once when I was young enough that the memory has the quality of a dream. The lake was small and green and perfectly still. He rowed us out in an aluminum boat that leaked from somewhere under the seat. I remember his hands on the oars and the sound of the water and a heron standing in the shallows like a piece of gray paper folded into the shape of a bird. We did not catch anything. He told me a story about his own father that I cannot remember no matter how hard I try. I know it was important because of the way he told it, leaning forward with his elbows on his knees, looking at the water instead of at me. The boat leaked slowly the whole time. By the time we rowed back my shoes were wet. He died the following spring.

title: The Office
text: Eleven years at the same desk in the same building. A window that does not open. Gray carpet. Walls the color of nothing. Lunch at the desk every day, a sandwich, an apple, a can of something. The woman in the next cubicle has a photograph of a beach pinned above her monitor. Sometimes in meetings you count the ceiling tiles. Same number every time. You count them anyway. Not unhappy. Not happy. There. Eleven years. When you leave they give you a card signed by people you saw every day whose handwriting you do not recognize.

title: Night Drive
text: We drove through the desert at night with the windows down and the radio playing something old and slow. The road was straight for so long that the headlights seemed to reach all the way to the horizon. She had her bare feet on the dashboard and was singing along quietly, getting most of the words wrong. The stars were absurd, like someone had spilled them. Every twenty minutes a truck would pass going the other way and the cab light would flash across her face. I was tired but did not want to stop because stopping meant arriving and arriving meant the trip was over. She fell asleep around midnight and I drove for another two hours in the dark, alone with the engine and the road and the enormous quiet sky. I have never been that content since.

title: The Hospital
text: The room smells like plastic and something chemical that is supposed to smell like lemons. The patient is smaller than expected. The machines make sounds that become ignorable and then cannot be stopped from being heard. His hands rest on top of the blanket, palms up, as though waiting to receive something. The nurse comes every hour. She is efficient and kind in the way that people who see death every day are kind, which is to say honestly. The daughter sits in the chair by the window reading a book she will not remember. The son arrives at six and they stand in the hallway discussing practical matters. The lawyer. The house. The dog. The patient dies at four in the morning while both children sleep in chairs on either side of the bed. The machine makes a sound and then it stops and the room is quieter than any room either of them has ever been in.

title: The Garden
text: Every year she grows tomatoes in the same raised bed he built before the child was born. The wood is rotting. She refuses to replace it. She is out there at dawn in her bathrobe, coffee in one hand, pulling weeds with the other. The tomatoes are never very good. They split in the rain. The birds get to them. She refuses to use spray. But every August she brings them inside in a colander and sets them on the counter and looks at them like jewels. She makes sauce that takes all day. The kitchen windows fog up and the whole house smells like summer. She dies in February. When August comes he goes to the garden and the bed is full of weeds and the wood has finally collapsed on one side. He stands there for a while. Then he goes inside and makes sauce from canned tomatoes. It is not the same but he makes it anyway.

title: The Letter
text: I found the letter in a box in the closet after she moved out. It was written on hotel stationery from a place in Portland. The handwriting was not hers. I read it twice and then I folded it and put it back in the envelope and put the envelope back in the box and put the box back in the closet. I went to work. I came home. I made dinner and ate it standing at the counter. I did not open the box again. I thought about it every day for a year and then less often and then only when I opened that closet, which I learned to do without looking at the shelf where the box was. When I moved out of the apartment I put the box in a trash bag with old magazines. I do not know what the letter said exactly. I read it twice and I remember the feeling of reading it but the words are gone.

title: The Bus
text: The bus from Tulsa took fourteen hours and I was next to a man who was traveling to see his son for the first time in nine years. He told me this within the first five minutes. He told me the whole story, which was long and involved a woman and a court and a state line and a lot of silence. He had a photograph in his wallet that he showed me. The boy in the picture was about four. He would be thirteen now, the man said. He did not know what the boy looked like at thirteen. He did not know if the boy wanted to see him. The bus stopped in every town. At a gas station in Arkansas the man bought two candy bars and put one in his jacket pocket. For the boy, he said. I got off in Dallas and shook his hand and wished him well and watched the bus pull away. I think about him sometimes. I never learned his name.

title: Swimming
text: A public pool. Chlorine and band-aids. The instructor is a teenager who seems annoyed to be there. She holds the child under the arms and says kick. The child kicks and swallows water and cries. Three weeks of this. Then one Tuesday the child is floating. There is no memory of the transition. One moment drowning and the next lying on top of the water with the ceiling above and its long fluorescent lights and breathing happening on its own. The instructor says good and walks away. The child lies there a long time. The water holds the child up. All the sounds of the pool come through the water, muffled and strange and beautiful. The child does not want to stand up. The child wants to stay in that held weightless quiet forever.

title: The Roof
text: When I was seventeen I climbed onto the roof of the school at night with two people I no longer remember. One of them had stolen a bottle of wine from a parent. We sat on the flat tar surface near the heating units and passed the bottle and looked at the town. It was a small town and from the roof you could see all of it, the Main Street with its three bars and the church and the water tower and beyond that the fields going out flat to the edge of the sky. One of them said something about leaving and never coming back. The other said something about it not being so bad. I said nothing. The wine was warm and tasted like metal. Below us the parking lot was empty except for one car with its headlights on, just sitting there, engine running, going nowhere. I have been to many places since then. I did leave. But sometimes at night I am back on that roof, seventeen, with the whole dark town below me, and I have not yet made any of the choices that would bring me to where I am now.
